# Extração: 3.4

- **Fonte:** 3.4.pdf
- **Tipo:** PDF

---

Can I start from $5/d?
Yes. However you need a good number of impressions to be confident in the numbers. I aim
for 1000 impressions before making a decision and ideally 3-5K before I call it a winner,
knowing that the results are stable. These are only benchmark ranges. Also, aim to give your
ads at least 24 hours, if not 48 hours to avoid any false positives or chance performance.
Do also consider the Ocean, Boats and Hooks analogy because one bad ad in an ad set
may just be that the ad hit a bad patch of the audience.
So duplicating an ad a few times can help to PROPERLY test it. It's common to run 2-3
duplicates of an ad in an ad set for this reason.
What's the ideal budget?
I'd start with at least $10 per day per ad set, up to $25 per day per ad set. It's about getting
statistical relevance of IMPRESSIONS not how much you spend. Since PPE is *usually* 4-5
times cheaper than WC it means you can test 4-5 times faster with PPE.
Do I set an ad set or campaign budget?
Either options work. If you set a campaign budget (CBO) of $100 per day with 10 ad sets,
then Facebook will decide, based on performance, which ad set to give the budget to. It's a
good way of focussing budget on best performance.
However, for testing purposes, setting budgets at ad set level and monitoring gives you more
control to ensure the ad sets get sufficient spend to give you insights, which is my
preference.
As of the time of writing this CBO is not fully reliable on its own to manage budgets across
ad sets.
How do I call a winner?
I'm looking for CTR as my lead indicator for non video ads. I do look for CPC but it's
important to be aware this is a factor of CPM (cheaper in PPE) and CTR, so to avoid
confusion, focus on CTR and just keep an eye on CPC.
For Ecom I'm looking then at cost per View Content and (if any ATCs have happened) cost
per ATC. At small impressions of 1.5K-2K you might not get ATCs but I'm looking at my cost
per VC being within a decent range.
What's decent? It should be fairly close to your CPC since in most cases you're sending
traffic to your product page which has the VC pixel installed.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 4 --

For video I'm looking at cost per 10 second view (ideally kept below $0.10) and % watched
at 25% and how many get to 10 seconds.
Do I have to run testing in PPE only? Can I test in other Objectives?
I prefer using PPE campaigns because of how Facebook will prioritise those that are likely to
engage with ads from your audience, which is easier for the algorithm to find than people
that are likely to convert. It allow for smaller spends to go in and faster results to come out
from testing.
However there is nothing stopping you from testing with other objectives. For example with
Lead Gen and Mobile App Install Objectives, because they are, to some extent more similar
to PPE than Website Conversion, testing using these objectives can work as well as running
PPE.
Even if I have a product catalog ad or canvas, I still use Grad Testing in PPE to test
images/copy etc to then test in a Product Catalog campaign or WC.
The key concept here is that Grad Testing with PPE is based purely on fast iterative testing.
It's not the only way to test.
If testing in a different objective like Product Catalog, WC or Lead Gen, the same concept
applies except the costs will be higher.
For WC, if I'm in TEST MODE then I'd switch the optimised pixel to Landing Page Views so
I'm going wider with the audience to see which ads/ad sets match.
Then when GRADUATING you would duplicate and change the optimised pixel to Purchase
or IC as required.
You can keep the same ad set and switch the optimised pixel but this can also cause
problems since the history was built up with a different optimised pixel.
I'm testing interests. Do I separate them? Or combine?
When testing interests, I prefer to separate them into their own ad sets so I can isolate the
ones that are working or not. Check out the demo video to see my setup. Once I've qualified
them through testing I'll move into my main campaign and combine interests into a single ad
set if they're similar interests and requiring similar ads.
However if you have lets say 40-50 interests and don't want the hassle of testing each one
individually, group them by commonality.
It doesn make it more difficult to judge which one worked, and there are a few tools available
like Connectio that split click data by interest but for this reason my preference is to keep
them separate and test in batches.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 4 --

What if my Graduation Framework test doesn't generate sales/leads?
It's not intended to. Remember, all you are testing is whether an audience reacts positively
to an ad, measured in CTR and cost per VC. There are indicator metrics further down the
funnel you can analyse which are covered in the demo video.
I'm testing lookalikes (LLAs). Once tested do I keep them separate?
When testing LLAs keep them separate for Graduation Framework testing. So you might
have a 1% of all visitors, view content, add to cart etc - keep them all separate through
testing so you can see which ones work. When moving them to your main campaign, then
it's ok to stack the 1% audiences together. The reason for this is that if you have a group of
high performing 1% audiences, then a) you're potentially making the audience size bigger,
which works better with Facebook generally and b) you're squeezing the best of all
audiences and giving more juicy data to work with for the algorithm.
If however you're running a nested test, that is a 1%, 1-2%, 2-3% of a single custom
audience then again it is possible to combine, if you need larger audiences. If the 1% (which
for the US is over 1M generally) is working and so is the 2-3% for example then keeping
them separate allows you to manage your budget across the ad sets, increasing and
decreasing as you see fit. If you need a larger audience then merging them is fine (for
example in the UK where 1% is around 400K).
Facebook needs 500 impressions for Relevance Score and Ad Delivery - how do you
use this with Grad Testing?
> 500 impressions is important, but not the ONLY thing to worry about. Don't get hung up if
you're spending less than $500 per ad set per day.
> That said, if FB can figure out insights/relevance after 500 impressions it means there is
some statistical validity in this number
> I use the Oceans, Boats & Hooks analogy: Oceans = Audiences, Boats = Ad sets, Hooks =
Ads. Your campaign objective and ad set targeting defines where in the ocean the boat is
placed then the hooks are dropped in (ads) to find the fish (customers). In an ocean of fish,
ALL of them should eat from the bait but not all will. So you keep your boat moving.
Furthermore, some fish like worms, others like maggots. So you keep rotating your hooks in
the ocean you're in (creative rotation) because the ocean is plentiful but sometimes the bait
isn't right (angle).
> I don't judge an ad until min 1.5K impressions minimum (sometimes less if it's a real
stinker), ideally 2-3K impressions depending on funnel and growth stage. Focus on
impressions not spend as spend is at the mercy of CPM. I know FB's machines are happy
with 500 but I'm human, I need more data. So will you.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 4 --

> If I have a winning creative then I'm using that via shared post ID in my new ad set (moving
from PPE to WC, though not available for all ad types) to give it the best chance of a running
start (because social proof matters). The ad history will NOT carry into a new ad set but the
social proof will.
> If all I have is one winning creative then I'll dupe that same post ID multiple times in the ad
set.
> Yes launching an ad to a warm audience (fans, past customers) is a great way of building
quality likes/comments on your ad, but it won't help your ad set because switching from fans
to your cold audience will reset learning, so you've lost the advantage at ad set level of the
500 impressions to fans
> Going in with a high bid/budget works IF you've already pre-qualified that the audience
works. For example if I've scaled an ad set modestly by increasing budgets 20-30% per day
when performance is good, and I want to scale faster, I'd dupe it up the budget and go in
with manual bid testing.
> Also if I have found a winning ad set/ad combo in Grad Testing, shifting to a high budget
$100+ in WC is a faster way of validating it as long as the right optimised pixel and event is
set. This is what needs a little tweaking
There's STILL a lot more to it but this should give you more context to getting started
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
